pattern: Every integer up to half the target can be used freely without ever summing to the target, so the cheapest beautiful array fills that whole safe range first and only then jumps past the forbidden zone to keep growing.
hints: Any two distinct integers that are both at most half of the target can never add up to the target, so the smallest floor(target/2) positive integers are always safe to use together.

Once you have used every integer in that safe zone, every integer from there up to just below the target is forbidden, because each one pairs with an already-used smaller integer to hit the target exactly.
If you still need more numbers after exhausting the safe zone, resume counting from the target itself upward, since nothing at or above the target can pair with anything smaller to reach it.

edge_inputs.description: small n fits entirely inside the safe zone plus one jump
n requires two numbers beyond the safe zone
the smallest possible n and target
n fits exactly at the edge of the safe zone
n and target both moderately large, still oracle-checkable
an even target where the exact midpoint is safe to use alone
n much larger than the safe zone forces most numbers to come from beyond the target

stress.description: random n and target across a moderate range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(2, size))
    target = rng.randint(1, max(2, size))
    return [n, target]
stress.note: moderate scale; the closed-form formula is what actually needs to hold at the full 10^9 boundary

stress.description: n and target pushed toward the full constraint boundary
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, size)
    target = rng.randint(1, size)
    return [n, target]
stress.note: exercises the modulo reduction at the full 10^9 scale

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minimumPossibleSum(self, n, target):
        # Independent implementation: directly simulate the greedy
        # construction (skip any value whose complement is already chosen)
        # instead of using the closed-form safe-zone formula.
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        chosen = set()
        total = 0
        v = 1
        count = 0
        while count < n:
            if (target - v) not in chosen:
                chosen.add(v)
                total += v
                count += 1
            v += 1
        return total % MOD
